summary: Eugene Stern (guest posting) dismantles Nick Kristof's New York Times op-ed claiming American students are failing at math compared to other countries. By examining the actual 2011 TIMSS data, Stern shows that US eighth graders placed 9th out of 42 countries—solidly in the top third. Kristof's alarming examples were cherry-picked: he selected the two questions out of 88 on which the US performed worst to support his predetermined narrative. The post demonstrates how punditry can manipulate statistics to manufacture a crisis that doesn't exist, while ironically ignoring the same standards of numeracy Kristof preaches.
key_points: US eighth graders actually ranked 9th out of 42 countries on the 2011 TIMSS, placing solidly in the top third
Kristof cherry-picked the 2 worst-performing questions out of 88 to manufacture a false narrative of American failure
American students excelled at statistics, data interpretation, and estimation—outperforming Russia, Finland, Japan, and Israel on some questions
The column offers no constructive suggestions while lecturing about the importance of numeracy
Pundits who moralize about statistical literacy often abuse statistics themselves to support predetermined narratives
money_quotes: The trouble with this narrative is that it's utterly, demonstrably false.
Kristof literally went and picked the two questions out of 88 on which the US did the worst, and highlighted those in the column.
Instead of a story about kids learning stuff and doing decently on a test, we have yet another hysterical screed about Americans 'struggling to compete with citizens of other countries.'
Kristof gives no suggestions for what we can actually do better, by the way.
He has a narrative of American underperformance in mind, and if the overall test results don't fit his story, he'll just go and find some results that do!
key_insight: Pundits who lecture about statistical literacy often cherry-pick data to support predetermined narratives, committing the very sins they condemn.
